Name: adaptive_rate_distortion_compression
Title: Adaptive Rate-Distortion Compression for Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: Adaptive bit allocation based on mutual information metrics can enhance model compression without compromising predictive sufficiency, providing a deeper understanding of the trade-offs between model size and performance.
Related Work: Existing works primarily focus on heuristic methods for model compression, such as adaptive mixed-precision quantization (SNIP) and training-free proxies (EMQ). However, they often lack a solid theoretical basis connecting the compression strategies to fundamental limits defined by rate-distortion theory, making them less optimal. This proposal distinctly aims to establish a rigorous framework that links mutual information metrics with adaptive bit allocation, thus filling the theoretical gap.
Abstract: In the quest for efficient neural network deployment, model compression techniques have gained prominence yet often rely on heuristic methods that lack theoretical grounding. This proposal introduces 'Adaptive Rate-Distortion Compression', a novel framework that leverages mutual information metrics to inform adaptive bit allocation for model compression. By framing model compression as a rate-distortion problem, we derive a theoretical foundation for optimal bit allocation, ensuring that the preserved information aligns with task relevance. We will validate our approach through empirical evaluations against existing state-of-the-art models, demonstrating improvements in compression efficiency while maintaining or enhancing predictive performance. The framework will provide fresh insights into the interplay between model capacity, distortion, and information retention, ultimately pushing the boundaries of what is achievable in model compression.
Experiments: 1. Develop a mutual information-based metric for assessing task-relevant information in neural network representations.
2. Implement an adaptive bit allocation algorithm that dynamically adjusts quantization levels based on the derived mutual information metrics.
3. Evaluate the proposed framework on benchmark datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) by comparing compression rates and predictive performance against existing methods (e.g., SNIP, EMQ).
4. Utilize metrics such as accuracy, F1-score, and compression ratio to assess performance.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed adaptive algorithm may introduce additional computational overhead during the training phase.
Mutual information estimation may be challenging, leading to potential inaccuracies that affect performance.
The theoretical bounds may not fully capture all scenarios encountered in practical applications.
